old_claim.note: Computers (Mainframe/Early) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Practical CT required enough computer capacity to reconstruct images from multiple X-ray measurements.

source_shape.source_locator: Nobel 1979 press-release description of computerized transverse axial tomography and practical computer processing.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames CT as computerized transverse axial tomography and describes the technical achievement of reconstructing cross-sectional images from many measurements.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports an enabling edge because computing is not merely historical background; it is the reconstruction capability that made practical CT imaging possible.

ontology_before: CT's computer edge was typed as a generic historical predecessor, making the reconstruction role less clear.
ontology_after: CT now treats computers as an enabling reconstruction technology, separate from the hard X-ray modality input.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: CT's computer edge is now an enabling dependency.
would_reject_if: The node were scoped to pre-computer mathematical tomography rather than practical medical CT scanners.
A source showed clinical CT reconstruction did not require computer processing.
The graph changed computers into a hard component edge while losing the X-ray foundation.
short_reason: Practical medical CT depends on computer reconstruction, not merely historical computing context.
